---
title: "Research"
page-layout: full
---

My research develops mathematical structures, dynamical viewpoints, information-theoretic ideas, and computational frameworks for complex systems. I am interested in systems in which local interactions, contextual constraints, evolving networks, and multi-level organisation jointly shape global behaviour.

A common thread across my work is the question of how local descriptions become coherent at the system level. This question appears in quantum foundations, quantum information and computation, topology-driven models of computation, evolving communication networks, and mathematical/theoretical biology. In each case, the central issue is not only how individual components behave, but how structure, constraints, information, and admissible transformations interact across an organised system.

My work sits at the interface of quantum foundations, quantum information and computation, applied topology, network science, and mathematical/theoretical biology.

---

## Unifying Questions

Across these areas, I am guided by a set of recurring questions:

- How do local interactions, measurements, or constraints assemble into coherent system-level behaviour?

- When does local consistency fail to produce a global model, as in contextuality, non-locality, or constrained network and biological systems?

- How can topology, geometry, algebra, and stability reveal emergent structures, invariants, and transitions in complex systems?

- How do computation, information, and structure interact in quantum, biological, and networked systems?

- What mathematical models are appropriate when the underlying network, state space, or space of admissible transformations changes over time?

- How can biological and communication systems be modelled when their relevant levels, interfaces, and constraints are part of the dynamics rather than fixed background data?

These questions connect my work on quantum contextuality, topology-driven computation, evolving networks, and multi-level biological organisation.

---

## Research Areas

The research areas below are organised into three layers: foundational physics, mathematical and computational structures, and complex systems and applications.

---

## Foundational Physics

::: {.grid}

::: {.g-col-12 .g-col-md-6}
::: {.card .h-100}
::: {.card-body}

### Quantum Foundations

I study non-locality, contextuality, Bell–Kochen–Specker models, and philosophical questions in quantum theory through topological, geometric, algebraic, and sheaf/bundle methods.

A central interest is the mathematical structure of contextuality: situations where local descriptions are meaningful, but cannot be assembled into a single globally consistent account. This perspective connects quantum foundations with local-to-global obstruction, symmetry principles, gauge-theoretic structure, and loop/interface holonomy.

[Explore this area →](quantum-foundations.qmd)

:::
:::
:::

::: {.g-col-12 .g-col-md-6}
::: {.card .h-100}
::: {.card-body}

### Quantum Information and Computation

I am interested in the mathematical structures behind quantum advantage and the role of contextuality as a computational resource. This direction connects quantum information, quantum computation, topology-driven computation, computational semantics, and foundational questions about quantum-causal structure.

More broadly, I study models of computation in which structure, constraints, and computation interact. In such systems, local computational processes are shaped by global organisation, while computation can also modify observable structures, invariants, or global organisation. This motivates a search for structural foundations of quantum algorithms and new forms of computation over changing spaces.

[Explore this area →](quantum-information-computation.qmd)

:::
:::
:::

:::

---

## Mathematical and Computational Structures

::: {.grid}

::: {.g-col-12 .g-col-md-6}
::: {.card .h-100}
::: {.card-body}

### Topological, Geometric, and Algebraic Methods

Across my work, I use mathematical structures from algebraic topology, sheaf theory, fibre bundles, obstruction theory, discrete Morse theory, category-theoretic ideas, and gauge/holonomy methods.

These tools provide a language for studying local-to-global structure: how local data are glued, transported, constrained, or obstructed across a larger system. They appear in my work on quantum contextuality, evolving networks, biological organisation, and topology-driven computation.

I am also interested in further mathematical directions where topology, geometry, algebra, and dynamics meet, including Morse–Floer–Conley theory, Hodge theory, topos theory, quantum topology, braid-theoretic structures, quantum cohomology, representation theory, and non-commutative geometry. These theories are important for understanding stability, invariants, transitions, and non-local structure in mathematical physics, computation, and complex systems.

[Explore this area →](topological-geometric-algebraic-methods.qmd)

:::
:::
:::

::: {.g-col-12 .g-col-md-6}
::: {.card .h-100}
::: {.card-body}

### Models of Computation

A further direction of my work concerns models of computation in which the underlying structure is not merely a passive substrate. I am interested in computational settings where data, constraints, topology, and admissible transformations interact, so that computation is shaped by the global organisation of the system.

This perspective appears in my work on contextual semantics machines and topology-driven models of computation. The guiding idea is that computation may involve not only state transitions on a fixed space, but also structural changes in the space of possibilities, the emergence of global observables, and the interaction between local processes and non-local invariants.

This direction connects quantum contextuality, quantum advantage, topological methods, and complex systems. It motivates the search for mathematical models of computation in which global structure, obstruction, stability, and emergence play an explicit role.

[Explore this area →](models-of-computation.qmd)

:::
:::
:::

:::

---

## Complex Systems and Applications

::: {.grid}

::: {.g-col-12 .g-col-md-6}
::: {.card .h-100}
::: {.card-body}

### Evolving Networks and Communication Systems

I study evolving, heterogeneous, and reconfigurable networked systems, especially communication systems where topology, configuration spaces, constraints, and admissible transformations change together.

The central problem is dynamic feasibility. A path, update, policy, or reconfiguration may appear possible at the network level, but its feasibility can depend on hidden constraints, compatibility between local configurations, boundary conditions, and the changing structure of the system.

I am especially interested in applications to strategic deployment, where decisions must be made under partial network information, resource constraints, and evolving operational conditions. This includes mathematical models for communication infrastructures, reconfigurable networks, and networked systems whose structure changes over time.

[Explore this area →](evolving-networks-communication-systems.qmd)

:::
:::
:::

::: {.g-col-12 .g-col-md-6}
::: {.card .h-100}
::: {.card-body}

### Mathematical and Theoretical Biology

I work on mathematical and theoretical models of multi-level biological processes, biological communication, circular causality, active interfaces, and adaptive ecological systems.

This direction studies biological systems whose relevant levels, interfaces, constraints, histories, and admissible transformations cannot always be reduced to one fixed state space. I am interested in how local biological processes become coherent system-level events, and how structural change affects what the system can do.

This line is connected to theoretical biology and the epistemology of biological organisation, including work associated with Giuseppe Longo and collaborators on biological time, organismal organisation, changing phase spaces, and the limits of purely mechanistic or fixed-state descriptions of living systems.

[Explore this area →](mathematical-theoretical-biology.qmd)

:::
:::
:::

:::





## Related Pages

::: {.grid}

::: {.g-col-12 .g-col-md-4}
::: {.card .h-100}
::: {.card-body}

### [Publications](publications.qmd)

Peer-reviewed publications, theses, preprints, and public manuscripts.

:::
:::
:::

::: {.g-col-12 .g-col-md-4}
::: {.card .h-100}
::: {.card-body}

### [Projects and Funding](projects.qmd)

Research environments, funding context, and ongoing projects.

:::
:::
:::

::: {.g-col-12 .g-col-md-4}
::: {.card .h-100}
::: {.card-body}

### [Contact](contact.qmd)

Affiliation, email, and academic links.

:::
:::
:::

:::
